elaboration.expanded_explanation:
ULTRAVIOLET (UV) SKIN HAZARDS

UV-A (315-400 nm):
- Penetrates to dermis
- Contributes to photoaging
- Minimal erythema (redness)
- Photosensitization with certain medications
- Cumulative effects over time

UV-B (280-315 nm):
- Partially absorbed by epidermis
- Causes sunburn (erythema)
- Tanning response
- DNA damage
- Skin cancer risk with chronic exposure

UV-C (100-280 nm):
- Strongly absorbed by stratum corneum
- High photon energy
- Used for sterilization
- Photokeratitis risk (eyes)
- Can cause severe surface burns

ACUTE UV EXPOSURE EFFECTS:
- Erythema (redness) - delayed 2-24 hours
- Edema (swelling)
- Pain and tenderness
- Blistering (severe exposure)
- Desquamation (peeling)

CHRONIC UV EXPOSURE EFFECTS:
- Photoaging (wrinkles, solar elastosis)
- Actinic keratosis (precancerous)
- Skin cancer (basal cell, squamous cell, melanoma)
- Immunosuppression
analogies: The eye's focusing capability is like a magnifying glass concentrating sunlight - it amplifies laser power by 100,000x at the retina.
Laser skin burns are similar to thermal burns but can be more precisely localized.
safety_implications: Always wear appropriate laser eyewear when working with Class 3B or Class 4 lasers.
Use protective gloves and long sleeves when operating high-power lasers.
UV laser exposure has cumulative effects similar to sun damage - use full coverage PPE.
